text.title: Design Tokens Implementation Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the Design Tokens Implementation methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: tokens.json MUST validate against a JSON Schema (W3C Design Tokens spec or local schema). Hand-edited tokens without validation are rejected.
rule.rationale: Schema validation catches naming typos that snake through reviews.

rule.statement: Build pipeline MUST produce byte-identical outputs given identical inputs. Non-determinism (timestamps in headers, random ordering) is rejected.
CI MUST run the build and diff against committed outputs; mismatches block merge.
If Figma is in the loop, the sync direction (Figma → code, code → Figma, both) MUST be documented + tooled (Tokens Studio plugin or similar).
rule.rationale: Ambiguous direction = both sides edit and clobber each other.

rule.statement: Build pipeline tests assert alias tokens resolve to base values; broken aliases are rejected.
rule.rationale: Silent alias breakage is the #2 token-system regression.

rule.statement: PRs that change tokens MUST update CHANGELOG with semver bump (token rename = major, value tweak = minor, new token = minor).
rule.rationale: Consumers depend on token stability; un-documented changes break them.

rule.statement: When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running Design Tokens Implementation under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
